title: Recognize an Emergency Situation
summary: Recognize when a situation is a true emergency that requires calling for help.
learnerPromise: You will be able to identify warning signs that indicate a situation is an emergency and needs immediate professional help.
whyItMatters: Knowing when a situation is beyond what you can handle helps you get the right help faster, which can improve the outcome for everyone involved.
realLifeUses: Deciding whether a bad fall needs an ambulance or just rest and ice
Recognizing signs of a severe allergic reaction after a bee sting
Knowing when chest pain or breathing trouble means calling emergency services
Reacting quickly when someone is unconscious and not responding
materialsNeeded: Optional: printed or digital list of emergency warning signs from a trusted health source
result: You can list at least five warning signs of a medical emergency and explain when to call for help.
youWillLearn: Identify common warning signs that suggest a medical emergency
Distinguish between a problem you can manage yourself and one that needs urgent help
Know the correct emergency number for your location
Stay calm enough to describe the situation and location to emergency responders
miniChallenge: Read a list of emergency warning signs from a trusted source. Write down five signs that would make you call for emergency help immediately.
steps: Look up the emergency warning signs list from a trusted medical or government source.
Read through the signs carefully, such as trouble breathing, severe bleeding, or chest pain.
Think about situations where you might see each sign and what you would do.
Write down five signs that you would treat as an emergency.
Confirm the emergency number for your area and save it in your phone.
completionCriteria: You can name at least five warning signs that require calling emergency services
You can explain the difference between a minor issue and a true emergency
You know the emergency number for your location
commonProblems: It can be hard to think clearly during a scary or chaotic moment
You might worry about overreacting and hesitate to call for help
Symptoms can look different in different people, especially children or older adults
tips: When in doubt, it is usually better to call and let the dispatcher guide you
Practice saying your address out loud so you can say it clearly under stress
Review the warning signs regularly so they stay fresh in your memory
helpBoundary: Call your local emergency number immediately if you or someone nearby has trouble breathing, severe bleeding, chest pain, signs of a severe allergic reaction, is unconscious, or you feel the situation is life-threatening. Do not wait to finish this skill first.
